sections.plain_summary: Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is a chronic inflammatory skin condition causing patches of dry, itchy, inflamed skin that flare and remit over time. It is driven by a combination of a defective skin barrier, immune dysregulation, and environmental triggers. It often begins in childhood but can persist into adulthood or first appear in adults. It frequently coexists with asthma, hay fever, and food allergies as part of what clinicians call the 'atopic march'.
sections.detailed_summary:
Atopic dermatitis is one of the most common chronic inflammatory conditions worldwide, affecting both children and adults. The skin barrier in affected individuals often has reduced levels of filaggrin — a structural protein that helps maintain the outer layer of skin — allowing allergens, irritants, and microbes to penetrate more easily. This triggers an immune response dominated by Th2 cytokines (particularly IL-4, IL-13, and IL-31), leading to inflammation, itching, and further barrier disruption in a self-reinforcing cycle.

Flares can be triggered by a wide range of factors: environmental allergens, contact irritants, sweat, heat, cold, stress, certain foods, skin infections (particularly Staphylococcus aureus), and hormonal changes. Itching is not just a symptom — it is a central driver of disease, because scratching damages the skin barrier further, inviting more inflammation and infection.

Many individuals with atopic dermatitis also have elevated IgE levels and a personal or family history of asthma and allergic rhinitis, reflecting a shared atopic immune phenotype. The condition is heterogeneous: some people experience primarily dry, scaly patches; others have weeping, crusted lesions; some have predominantly flexural involvement; others have widespread disease. In adults, hand eczema, eyelid involvement, and nummular (coin-shaped) patterns are common presentations that may be misdiagnosed or undertreated.

Management in mainstream dermatology focuses heavily on symptom control — emollients, topical corticosteroids, and newer targeted biologics. However, identifying and addressing individual triggers, underlying immune drivers, gut-skin axis factors, and food sensitivities may offer additional benefit for some people, and this is an area where self-directed investigation and integrative practitioners can add value that standard care often does not provide.

prevalence.summary: Atopic dermatitis is one of the most common chronic skin conditions globally, affecting a large proportion of children and a significant minority of adults.
prevalence.estimate: 15–20% of children and 1–10% of adults worldwide; approximately 7% of adults in the US
prevalence.notes: Prevalence has increased significantly in industrialised countries over the past several decades, possibly linked to the hygiene hypothesis, microbiome changes, and dietary shifts. Adult-onset atopic dermatitis is more common than previously thought and may be underdiagnosed. Prevalence varies considerably by geography, urbanisation, and ethnicity.

subgroups.name: Childhood-onset atopic dermatitis
subgroups.description: The classic presentation, beginning in infancy or early childhood, often on the face and extensor surfaces before shifting to flexural areas. Many children see improvement by adolescence, though a proportion carry the condition into adulthood.
distinguishing_features: Facial and scalp involvement common in infants
Flexural (elbow creases, behind knees) distribution in older children
Strongly associated with food allergies, asthma, and allergic rhinitis
High rates of filaggrin gene mutations in this group

subgroups.name: Adult-onset atopic dermatitis
subgroups.description: First presentation of atopic dermatitis in adults, which may appear de novo without childhood history. Often more severe and persistent, with different distribution patterns.
distinguishing_features: Head, neck, and hand involvement more prominent
May present as nummular (coin-shaped) or lichenified plaques
Often associated with significant psychological burden and sleep disruption
May have less obvious atopic family history
IgE may be less elevated than childhood-onset

subgroups.name: Intrinsic (non-IgE-mediated) atopic dermatitis
subgroups.description: A subgroup with the clinical features of atopic dermatitis but without elevated IgE or sensitisation to allergens. Thought to represent around 20–30% of cases.
distinguishing_features: Normal or low total IgE
Negative allergy skin prick tests
Less likely to have comorbid asthma or allergic rhinitis
More common in adults and women
May be missed by standard allergy workup

subgroups.name: Severe / treatment-refractory atopic dermatitis
subgroups.description: Individuals whose disease does not adequately respond to standard topical therapies and requires systemic treatment. May have persistent widespread involvement and markedly impaired quality of life.
distinguishing_features: EASI or IGA scores in severe range
Recurrent skin infections (especially S. aureus)
Severe sleep disruption and psychological comorbidity
Candidates for dupilumab or other biologics / JAK inhibitors
Often have multiple trigger categories simultaneously

subgroups.name: Food-triggered atopic dermatitis
subgroups.description: A subset — particularly prominent in young children — where specific foods (most commonly egg, milk, wheat, soy, peanut, and fish) act as drivers of flares.
distinguishing_features: More common in children under 5 with moderate-to-severe disease
Food triggers may be IgE-mediated (immediate) or non-IgE-mediated (delayed)
Standard allergy testing may miss delayed reactions
Dietary elimination under supervision can be diagnostic and therapeutic
Risk of nutritional inadequacy if multiple foods are eliminated without guidance

subgroups.name: Contact trigger-dominant atopic dermatitis
subgroups.description: Individuals whose atopic dermatitis is significantly driven or worsened by contact allergens (e.g. fragrances, preservatives, nickel, rubber chemicals) rather than aeroallergens or foods alone.
distinguishing_features: Concurrent allergic contact dermatitis identified on patch testing
Distribution may follow areas of contact with specific products
Often misattributed to atopic dermatitis alone without patch testing
Removal of the contact allergen can produce dramatic improvement

symptoms.notes: Often described as the defining and most debilitating feature. Can be worse at night, disrupting sleep. Scratching worsens the barrier and perpetuates the itch-scratch cycle.
Present even in non-flaring areas. Reflects baseline barrier dysfunction. Particularly noticeable in winter or low-humidity environments.
Can range from mild pink to deep red. In darker skin tones, may appear purple, grey, or brown rather than red, and is frequently under-documented in clinical literature.
Indicates active inflammation or secondary bacterial infection, particularly with Staphylococcus aureus. Yellow crusting is a common sign of S. aureus colonisation.
Develops in areas of chronic scratching or rubbing. Common at flexural sites in long-standing disease.
Linear scratch marks, often with small cuts or scabs. Can become infected.
More common in adult-onset disease. Can be misdiagnosed as ringworm or psoriasis.
Eyelid eczema is particularly common in adults and can be associated with contact allergens in skincare or eye products. May cause redness, swelling, and scaling around the eyes.
Very common in adult atopic dermatitis. Can be occupationally disabling. Often worsened by frequent handwashing, wet work, or contact with irritants.
Nocturnal itching is a major driver of poor sleep quality. Chronic sleep deprivation compounds psychological burden and may worsen immune dysregulation.

Rates of anxiety and depression are significantly elevated in people with atopic dermatitis, both as consequences of chronic itch and sleep loss, and possibly as part of shared neuroimmune mechanisms.
Often overlooked. Inflamed skin can be painful or burning, particularly during or after flares. More common in adults than children.

Compromised barrier increases susceptibility. S. aureus colonises nearly all moderate-to-severe eczema skin. Eczema herpeticum (widespread herpes simplex infection) is a rare but serious complication requiring urgent treatment.

symptoms.symptom: Dennie-Morgan lines (extra fold under the eye)
symptoms.notes: A minor physical sign associated with atopic disease. Not diagnostic alone but can support clinical picture.

symptoms.symptom: Keratosis pilaris (rough bumps on upper arms or thighs)
symptoms.notes: Frequently co-occurs with atopic dermatitis, reflecting shared barrier and keratinisation abnormalities.

key_patterns: Clinical diagnosis based on characteristic distribution and morphology, chronic or relapsing course, and personal or family history of atopy — no single test confirms the diagnosis
Elevated total IgE with specific IgE sensitisation to aeroallergens or foods in extrinsic (allergic) atopic dermatitis
Normal IgE with typical clinical features suggests intrinsic subtype — standard allergy tests may be negative
Positive patch testing (not prick testing) to contact allergens in individuals where contact triggers are suspected
Skin swab culture showing heavy S. aureus colonisation in cases of recurrent infection or crusting
Filaggrin (FLG) gene mutation in individuals with severe, early-onset, or persistent disease — associated with higher risk of food allergy and asthma

at_home.name: Symptom and trigger diary
at_home.description: Tracking flare timing, severity, location, and potential triggers (foods eaten, products used, environments visited, stress levels, sleep) in a daily diary can reveal patterns that are not obvious without systematic recording. At least 4–6 weeks of data is more informative than shorter periods.
at_home.safety_notes: No safety risk. The diary is a tool for identifying patterns, not for making elimination decisions alone.

at_home.name: Product elimination trial
at_home.description: Switching to fragrance-free, preservative-free, dye-free laundry detergent, and using only a simple unperfumed emollient for 4–6 weeks can help identify whether skincare or household products are contributing to flares. Reintroducing products one at a time afterwards can identify specific culprits.
at_home.safety_notes: Low risk. If skin worsens significantly during the trial, consult a dermatologist or allergist.

at_home.name: Supervised food elimination trial
at_home.description: For individuals (particularly children) who notice flares consistently after certain foods, a short-term elimination of the suspected food (typically 2–4 weeks) followed by careful reintroduction may help identify food triggers. This is most informative when done systematically with notes on skin response.
at_home.safety_notes: Elimination of multiple food groups without professional guidance carries nutritional risk, especially in children. Reintroduction of a food that caused a prior severe reaction should only be done under clinical supervision. Consult a dietitian or allergist before eliminating major food groups.

at_home.name: Wet wrap and emollient response assessment
at_home.description: Observing whether consistent twice-daily emollient application significantly reduces dryness and baseline itch between flares is informative — people with barrier-dominant disease often see meaningful improvement with emollients alone. Poor response suggests other drivers are more prominent.
at_home.safety_notes: Safe. Use emollients designed for sensitive or eczema-prone skin. Avoid fragranced products.

clinical.name: Clinical dermatological assessment
clinical.description: Diagnosis is primarily clinical, based on the UK Working Party or Hanifin & Rajka diagnostic criteria — assessing itch, distribution, morphology, chronicity, and atopic history. Validated severity scoring tools (EASI, SCORAD, IGA) are used to quantify disease burden.
clinical.availability: Dermatologist, or experienced GP. Severity scoring may not be routinely performed in primary care.

clinical.name: Total IgE and specific IgE (ImmunoCAP / RAST)
clinical.description: Blood tests measuring overall IgE levels and sensitisation to specific allergens (foods, dust mites, animal dander, moulds, pollens). Sensitisation does not always mean clinical relevance — results must be interpreted alongside symptoms.
clinical.availability: Ordered by GPs, allergists, or dermatologists. Available through most hospital and private labs.

clinical.name: Skin prick testing
clinical.description: Small amounts of allergen extracts are introduced via a lancet to the forearm skin. A wheal-and-flare response at 15 minutes indicates IgE-mediated sensitisation. Most useful for aeroallergens and immediate food reactions.
clinical.availability: Performed by allergists. Not routinely available in general practice.

clinical.name: Patch testing (epicutaneous testing)
clinical.description: Patches containing a standard series of contact allergens (fragrances, preservatives, metals, rubber chemicals, etc.) are applied to the back for 48 hours and read at 48 and 96 hours. Identifies type IV (delayed) contact hypersensitivity, which can coexist with and complicate atopic dermatitis. Particularly important in adults with facial, hand, or treatment-refractory disease.
clinical.availability: Performed by dermatologists or allergists with specialist patch testing facilities. Not universally available — may require referral to a specialist centre.

clinical.name: Skin swab for bacterial culture
clinical.description: Swabs of infected or acutely flaring areas can identify bacterial pathogens (most commonly S. aureus) and their antibiotic sensitivity. Useful when infection is suspected or when standard treatments are failing.
clinical.availability: Can be ordered by GPs or dermatologists. Processed by standard microbiology labs.

clinical.name: Filaggrin (FLG) gene mutation testing
clinical.description: Genetic testing for loss-of-function mutations in the FLG gene, which are present in approximately 30–40% of European patients with atopic dermatitis. FLG mutations are associated with earlier onset, greater severity, and higher risk of food allergy and asthma. Not yet routine in clinical practice but available privately.
clinical.availability: Available through specialist genetics labs and some private genomic testing services. Not yet standard NHS or insurance-covered practice.

clinical.name: Oral food challenge (OFC)
clinical.description: The definitive test for food allergy — controlled, graded exposure to a suspected food allergen under clinical supervision. Used to confirm or rule out food allergy, particularly where sensitisation tests are positive but clinical relevance is uncertain.
clinical.availability: Performed by allergists, usually in a hospital or specialist allergy clinic. Not available in primary care.

related_conditions.name: Allergic rhinitis
related_conditions.explanation: Part of the 'atopic triad' alongside asthma and atopic dermatitis. Many individuals with atopic dermatitis develop allergic rhinitis, driven by the same Th2-skewed immune response and IgE sensitisation to aeroallergens.

related_conditions.name: IgE-mediated food allergy
related_conditions.explanation: Children with moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis have substantially elevated rates of IgE-mediated food allergies, particularly to egg, milk, peanut, and wheat. The impaired skin barrier may be a route of sensitisation to food antigens.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Mast cells are central mediators in atopic dermatitis, releasing histamine and other inflammatory mediators that drive itch and inflammation. Some individuals with treatment-refractory or unusually reactive atopic dermatitis may have underlying mast cell dysregulation consistent with MCAS.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Histamine released by mast cells drives itch in atopic dermatitis. Some individuals also have impaired histamine metabolism, and high-histamine foods may worsen symptoms. This overlap is under-recognised in standard dermatology.

related_conditions.name: Nickel sensitivity
related_conditions.explanation: Nickel contact allergy is common in the general population and particularly prevalent in atopic individuals. Systemic nickel allergy syndrome (SNAS) can cause widespread or hand eczema flares from dietary nickel, mimicking or compounding atopic dermatitis.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Alterations in the gut microbiome have been associated with atopic dermatitis, particularly in infants and children. The gut-skin axis hypothesis suggests that intestinal dysbiosis may promote systemic immune skewing toward Th2 responses. Probiotic research in this area is active but results are mixed.

related_conditions.name: Eosinophilic GI disorders
related_conditions.explanation: Eosinophilic oesophagitis and other eosinophilic GI disorders share the same Th2 immune environment as atopic dermatitis and occur at higher rates in atopic individuals. Some patients carry both conditions simultaneously.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Rates of anxiety are significantly elevated in people with atopic dermatitis, driven by chronic itch, sleep disruption, social stigma, and appearance concerns. Psychological stress also directly triggers or worsens flares via neuroimmune pathways.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depression is substantially more prevalent in people with atopic dermatitis than in the general population. The relationship is bidirectional — depression worsens disease management, and disease burden drives depressive symptoms.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: Epidemiological studies have found higher rates of ADHD in children with atopic dermatitis, possibly related to chronic sleep disruption, shared neuroimmune mechanisms, or common genetic risk factors.

related_conditions.name: Asthma
related_conditions.explanation: Part of the atopic march — atopic dermatitis in infancy is a significant risk factor for later development of asthma. Both conditions share Th2 immune skewing, and the same allergen sensitisations may drive both.

related_conditions.name: Allergic contact dermatitis
related_conditions.explanation: Allergic contact dermatitis (type IV hypersensitivity to contact allergens) can coexist with or mimic atopic dermatitis. The two conditions can be distinguished and identified by patch testing. Failure to identify contact triggers in atopic patients leads to inadequate treatment response.

related_conditions.name: Psoriasis
related_conditions.explanation: Psoriasis can present with red, scaly plaques that may resemble atopic dermatitis. Key differences include sharper borders, silvery scale, nail and joint involvement in psoriasis, and different distribution patterns. Distinction matters because treatments differ significantly.

related_conditions.name: Seborrhoeic dermatitis
related_conditions.explanation: Seborrhoeic dermatitis affects oily areas (scalp, face, chest) and can coexist with or be confused with atopic dermatitis, particularly in facial presentations. Driven by Malassezia yeast rather than atopic immune mechanisms.

related_conditions.name: Scabies
related_conditions.explanation: Scabies (mite infestation) causes intense itch, particularly at night, and may produce eczematous skin changes. It can be mistaken for atopic dermatitis and will not respond to eczema treatments. Should be considered particularly in new or rapidly worsening presentations.

related_conditions.name: Ichthyosis vulgaris
related_conditions.explanation: Ichthyosis vulgaris, caused by filaggrin mutations, frequently co-occurs with atopic dermatitis because of shared genetic risk. Both conditions involve skin barrier dysfunction. Individuals with both have more persistent and severe atopic dermatitis.

strategies.name: Regular, liberal emollient use
strategies.description: Applying a thick, fragrance-free emollient (moisturiser) to the entire body at least twice daily — and immediately after bathing while skin is still slightly damp — helps restore and maintain the skin barrier, reducing transepidermal water loss and decreasing the frequency and severity of flares. Ointments (e.g. white soft paraffin, Vaseline) provide better occlusion than creams for very dry or cracked skin; creams and lotions may be preferred for daytime use.
strategies.notes: Emollients should be viewed as daily maintenance, not just used during flares. Some individuals react to preservatives or fragrances in emollients — patch testing or trial of multiple products may be needed to find a tolerated formulation.

strategies.name: Topical corticosteroids (TCS)
strategies.description: The first-line anti-inflammatory treatment for active eczema flares. Applied to affected areas in the appropriate potency for the site (lower potency for face and groin; higher potency for thicker skin or lichenified areas). Used for short courses during flares, or as proactive (twice-weekly) therapy on previously affected areas to prevent relapse in moderate-to-severe disease.
strategies.notes: Long-term, inappropriate use of potent corticosteroids can cause skin thinning, striae, and systemic effects. Topical steroid withdrawal (TSW) is a contentious area — a subset of individuals report significant worsening upon discontinuation after prolonged use, though the condition is not yet uniformly recognised by dermatologists. Potency selection and application guidance from a dermatologist is important for longer-term use.

strategies.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus)
strategies.description: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory topical treatments that suppress T-cell activation in the skin. Particularly useful for sensitive areas (face, eyelids, groin) where long-term corticosteroid use is not appropriate, and for maintenance therapy. Pimecrolimus is lower potency; tacrolimus 0.1% is used for moderate-to-severe disease.
strategies.notes: A black-box warning regarding theoretical malignancy risk was added historically, but this has not been confirmed in long-term studies. Burning sensation on initial application is common and usually diminishes. Requires prescription.

strategies.name: Dupilumab (Dupixent) and other biologics
strategies.description: Dupilumab is a monoclonal antibody targeting IL-4 and IL-13 signalling, the central Th2 cytokines driving atopic dermatitis. Administered by fortnightly injection, it produces significant, sustained improvement in moderate-to-severe disease. JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, abrocitinib, baricitinib) are newer oral targeted therapies with rapid onset.
strategies.notes: Dupilumab is generally well-tolerated. Conjunctivitis is a notable side effect in some users. JAK inhibitors carry class warnings around infections, blood clots, and cardiovascular events. These are specialist-prescribed medications requiring dermatologist assessment and, in many healthcare systems, demonstration of prior treatment failure.

strategies.name: Trigger identification and avoidance
strategies.description: Systematically identifying and reducing exposure to personal flare triggers can significantly reduce disease burden. Common triggers include house dust mites, pet dander, pollen, sweat, wool clothing, fragranced products, certain foods, environmental moulds, tobacco smoke, and psychological stress. Not all triggers apply to all individuals.
strategies.notes: Trigger avoidance alone is rarely sufficient for moderate-to-severe disease but can meaningfully reduce medication burden. A symptom diary is essential for identifying patterns. Overly restrictive avoidance behaviour can impair quality of life without proportionate benefit.

strategies.name: House dust mite reduction measures
strategies.description: For individuals sensitised to house dust mite (confirmed by allergy testing), measures such as allergen-impermeable mattress and pillow covers, weekly hot washing of bedding (60°C or above), reducing soft furnishings in the bedroom, and acaricides on carpets may reduce antigenic load and skin exposure.
strategies.notes: Evidence for clinical benefit specifically in atopic dermatitis (as opposed to asthma or rhinitis) is moderate. Most benefit is seen in individuals with confirmed sensitisation rather than as a blanket approach.

strategies.name: Wet wrap therapy
strategies.description: Applying a topical treatment or emollient to the skin and then covering with a layer of damp bandages or tubular garments, followed by a dry layer. Used for acute flares, particularly in children. Increases absorption of topical treatments and provides a physical barrier against scratching.
strategies.notes: Most effective under clinical guidance. Prolonged use of wet wraps over topical corticosteroids increases systemic absorption. Best used as a short-term intensive measure.

strategies.name: Antihistamines for itch
strategies.description: Sedating antihistamines (e.g. hydroxyzine, chlorphenamine) may help with sleep disruption from nocturnal itch, though they do not address the underlying inflammation. Non-sedating antihistamines have limited evidence for itch relief in atopic dermatitis, as the itch is not primarily histamine-mediated in the same way as urticaria.
strategies.notes: The primary itch mediator in atopic dermatitis is IL-31 and neuropeptide signalling, not histamine alone — which is why antihistamines are less effective than in hives. Sedating antihistamines are better used short-term for sleep disruption. Long-term use of sedating antihistamines has cognitive effects, particularly in older adults.

strategies.name: Probiotics
strategies.description: Some studies suggest that specific probiotic strains (particularly Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG and certain Bifidobacterium strains) taken during pregnancy and/or early infancy may reduce the risk of atopic dermatitis in high-risk children. Evidence for benefit in established disease in older children and adults is weaker and inconsistent.
strategies.notes: Strain specificity matters significantly — general probiotic products are not equivalent to studied strains. Most evidence is in prevention rather than treatment. Some individuals notice improvement; others do not.

strategies.name: Psychological support and stress management
strategies.description: Psychological stress is a well-established trigger for atopic dermatitis flares via neuroimmune pathways. Structured stress management, cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR), and habit reversal training for the itch-scratch cycle have evidence for reducing disease burden in chronic eczema.
strategies.notes: The 'Combined Approach' developed by dermatologist Peter Stalder and psychologist Uwe Gieler integrates habit reversal with eczema education and has specific evidence in atopic dermatitis. Psychological support is underutilised in standard dermatology care.

strategies.name: Narrowband UVB phototherapy
strategies.description: Controlled exposure to narrowband UVB light suppresses cutaneous immune activity and can produce significant improvement in moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis. Typically delivered as two to three sessions per week in a dermatology clinic for 12–20 sessions.
strategies.notes: Requires regular clinic attendance. Cumulative UV exposure over many treatment courses may increase long-term skin cancer risk. Not suitable in pregnancy. Good option for those who cannot use or prefer to avoid systemic immunosuppressants.

strategies.name: Dietary investigation for food triggers
strategies.description: Supervised elimination and reintroduction of suspected food triggers, conducted with a registered dietitian or allergist, can identify foods that contribute to flares in a subset of individuals — particularly children with moderate-to-severe disease. Common suspects include dairy, egg, wheat, soy, fish, and peanuts.
strategies.notes: Not all people with atopic dermatitis have food triggers — this is most relevant in younger children and those with confirmed sensitisation. Broad dietary elimination without proper guidance can cause nutritional deficiencies and is not recommended as a first step.

strategies.name: Bathing practice optimisation
strategies.description: Short, lukewarm baths or showers (10 minutes or less) using a fragrance-free, soap-free cleanser or emollient wash, followed immediately by patting dry and applying emollient while skin is still slightly damp, can help maintain barrier function. Avoiding hot water and harsh soaps reduces stripping of natural skin oils.
strategies.notes: Frequency of bathing is debated — most guidelines recommend daily bathing followed by emollients rather than avoiding bathing. The key is the post-bath emollient application window.

strategies.name: Vitamin D supplementation
strategies.description: Vitamin D deficiency has been associated with increased atopic dermatitis severity and susceptibility to S. aureus colonisation. Supplementation may help some individuals, particularly those with documented deficiency or limited sun exposure.
strategies.notes: Evidence is stronger for deficient individuals than for those with normal levels. Testing serum 25-OH vitamin D before supplementing is worthwhile. Standard supplementation doses (400–2000 IU daily) are generally safe.

things_to_avoid: Fragranced skincare, soap, shampoo, and laundry products — fragrance is one of the most common contact sensitisers and irritants in eczema
Hot showers and baths — heat increases vasodilation and itch, and strips skin of protective oils
Wool or scratchy synthetic fabrics next to the skin — mechanical irritation triggers itch; loose, soft cotton or bamboo is better tolerated by most
Tobacco smoke exposure — a recognised irritant and immune trigger in atopic disease
Overheating and heavy sweating without prompt skin care — sweat can irritate eczema skin and trigger itch

Scratching without an alternative — breaks the skin barrier, introduces infection, worsens inflammation, and perpetuates the itch-scratch cycle; scratching substitutes (patting, cooling) are more helpful
Using multiple new skincare products simultaneously — makes it impossible to identify which product is causing a reaction
Prolonged use of high-potency topical corticosteroids on thin skin (face, genitals, skin folds) without medical supervision — risks skin atrophy and systemic absorption
Unnecessary or unsupervised elimination of multiple food groups — risks nutritional inadequacy without confirmed food trigger identification
Ignoring signs of skin infection (yellow crust, pus, worsening pain, fever) — infected eczema requires prompt treatment and will not respond to standard eczema management alone

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic condition that cannot currently be cured — management is about reducing burden and preventing flares, not elimination of the underlying immune tendency.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: People naturally seek a cure, and the internet is full of claims about dietary protocols or products that will 'clear eczema for good'. The cycle of hope and disappointment around promised cures causes significant distress and can lead to abandonment of genuinely effective management.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who reach the best long-term outcomes typically build a consistent daily routine (emollients, trigger management, proactive therapy) rather than chasing cures. Flares become less frequent, less severe, and more predictable. The disease may go into prolonged remission, particularly in childhood-onset disease, but the underlying tendency usually remains.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Most people need to apply emollient every single day — including when skin appears clear — not just during flares. Stopping when skin looks good is a major driver of relapse.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Daily emollient application is time-consuming and often feels unnecessary when skin is not actively inflamed. The benefit of barrier maintenance is invisible until flares become less frequent, making it easy to attribute good periods to chance rather than routine.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Individuals who integrate daily emollient use into their routine as non-negotiable habit maintenance — like brushing teeth — typically experience significantly longer flare-free periods and reduced overall medication burden.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Topical steroid phobia leads many people to undertreat their eczema and live with more inflammation and suffering than necessary. Used correctly, topical corticosteroids are safe and effective.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Widely circulated fears about skin thinning, steroid dependency, and systemic effects — amplified online — cause many people to use too little steroid, too infrequently, or stop prematurely. Some fear is warranted (for specific sites or prolonged misuse), but most people use too little rather than too much.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who work with a dermatologist to understand appropriate potency selection, correct application technique, and safe duration typically achieve better disease control with less total steroid exposure than those who undertreat and experience prolonged flares requiring higher-potency or more frequent courses.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Dietary elimination strategies are rarely the primary driver of improvement in most adults with atopic dermatitis, and extensive food restriction without confirmed triggers causes harm.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Food feels like something people can control, and the gut-skin axis narrative is compelling. Many individuals pursue increasingly restrictive diets hoping to find the trigger, which can lead to nutritional problems, disordered eating, and social impairment without proportionate skin benefit.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Individuals who undergo supervised allergy testing and structured elimination-reintroduction under dietitian guidance either identify genuine triggers (allowing targeted, not blanket, restriction) or rule out food as a primary driver — freeing them to focus on more impactful management strategies.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Psychological stress is not just a 'consequence' of eczema — it is a genuine biological trigger of flares through neuroimmune pathways, and ignoring the mental health dimension of the disease leaves a major lever unaddressed.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Acknowledging that stress worsens eczema can feel like being told the disease is 'in your head' or that it is a personal failure to manage emotions. Stress is also often not addressable quickly, making it an uncomfortable target.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who engage with structured psychological approaches — particularly habit reversal training for the itch-scratch cycle and stress management strategies — show measurable reductions in flare frequency and severity in clinical studies, alongside improved sleep and quality of life.

key_levers.lever: Daily emollient use as a non-negotiable routine
key_levers.why_it_matters: Barrier dysfunction is a central mechanism of atopic dermatitis. Regular emollient use measurably reduces transepidermal water loss, decreases inflammatory sensitisation, and extends flare-free intervals. It is the highest-impact low-risk intervention available.
key_levers.how_to_start: Choose a single fragrance-free, preservative-light emollient (an ointment or thick cream). Apply to the whole body within 3 minutes of bathing, every single day. Commit to this for 4 weeks and track flare frequency.
key_levers.timeline: Improvement in skin hydration is noticeable within days; reduction in flare frequency may take several weeks of consistent use

key_levers.why_it_matters: Atopic dermatitis is driven by a combination of individual triggers that vary between people. Identifying and reducing the most significant personal triggers can dramatically reduce the frequency of flares without requiring additional medication.
key_levers.how_to_start: Start a 4-week symptom and exposure diary, recording flare timing, severity, and potential triggers daily. Look for patterns across environment, products, foods, stress levels, and seasonal changes.
key_levers.timeline: Pattern identification typically requires 4–8 weeks of consistent logging

key_levers.lever: Optimising anti-inflammatory treatment to actually control flares
key_levers.why_it_matters: Many people undertreat their eczema due to fear of medication side effects, leading to prolonged inflammation that causes progressive barrier damage, lichenification, and higher risk of sensitisation. Adequate treatment of flares breaks the inflammation cycle more effectively.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult a dermatologist to review current topical treatment — correct potency selection, application technique, and treatment duration. Ask specifically about proactive (twice-weekly) maintenance therapy if flares recur at the same sites.
key_levers.timeline: Appropriate flare treatment should produce visible improvement within 7–14 days

key_levers.why_it_matters: Scratching is a behaviour that perpetuates the disease regardless of other management. Habit reversal training has specific evidence in atopic dermatitis for reducing scratch behaviour and the skin damage it causes, producing downstream reductions in infection risk and barrier disruption.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin by becoming aware of when and where scratching occurs (diary or phone notes). Identify substitutes for scratching (pressing firmly with flat hand, applying cool cloth, tapping). A clinical psychologist or dermatology nurse with CBT training can provide structured habit reversal therapy.
key_levers.timeline: Initial awareness of patterns within 2 weeks; meaningful behaviour change with structured habit reversal over 6–12 weeks

key_levers.why_it_matters: For individuals sensitised to specific aeroallergens or contact allergens, ongoing allergen exposure maintains a background of immune activation that makes eczema harder to control regardless of topical treatment. Reducing this background load can substantially lower disease burden.
key_levers.how_to_start: If not already done, arrange allergy testing (specific IgE blood tests, skin prick tests) and patch testing through a dermatologist or allergist. Act on confirmed sensitisations — mite covers, pet avoidance, fragrance elimination — rather than broad precautionary restrictions.
key_levers.timeline: Environmental changes may take weeks to months to show skin benefit; contact allergen avoidance can produce visible improvement within 4–6 weeks

key_levers.why_it_matters: Poor sleep and chronic stress directly worsen atopic dermatitis through neuroimmune mechanisms and impair the ability to maintain management routines. Addressing these dimensions simultaneously with skin-directed treatment produces better outcomes than either alone.
key_levers.how_to_start: Prioritise sleep environment (cool, light cotton bedding, trimmed nails to reduce nocturnal scratch damage). Seek referral to a psychologist with experience in chronic skin conditions or pain management if stress, anxiety, or depression is a prominent feature.
key_levers.timeline: Sleep improvement may be noticeable within weeks of effective flare control; psychological interventions typically show benefit over 8–16 weeks

common_mistakes: Using emollient only during flares and stopping when skin clears — this allows barrier to deteriorate again and flares to return more quickly
Applying emollient then immediately getting dressed, not allowing it to absorb and not maintaining a consistent routine
Using too little topical corticosteroid, applying it too infrequently, or stopping too soon due to fear — leading to prolonged undertreated inflammation
Eliminating multiple foods simultaneously without systematic reintroduction, making it impossible to determine which (if any) are genuine triggers
Switching products frequently trying to find the 'right' one — making it impossible to know what is helping or causing reactions
Treating eczema only reactively (treating flares) rather than proactively (maintaining barrier and preventing flares daily)
Ignoring psychological and sleep dimensions of the condition, which can undermine all other management efforts
Pursuing naturopathic or unregulated treatments that promise cure — these carry the risk of delaying effective treatment, applying sensitising ingredients, or causing contact dermatitis
Not seeking specialist review for facial, eyelid, or hand eczema — these sites require specific management approaches often not delivered adequately in primary care
Failing to get patch testing in treatment-refractory disease — missing a concurrent contact allergen means the underlying driver is never addressed

specialists.role: Primary specialist for diagnosis, severity assessment, selection of topical and systemic treatments including biologics, and coordination of patch testing. Can provide structured treatment plans and proactive maintenance protocols.
specialists.notes: In many countries there are long waits for NHS or insurance-funded dermatology. Private dermatology appointments for initial assessment and treatment plan are an option if waiting lists are prohibitive. Seek a dermatologist who takes time to discuss management strategy rather than simply prescribing topical steroids at each visit.

specialists.role: Conducts specific IgE testing, skin prick testing, patch testing, and oral food challenges. Essential for individuals with suspected food allergies, aeroallergen sensitisation, or contact allergies complicating atopic dermatitis. Can also assess for comorbid asthma and allergic rhinitis.
specialists.notes: Not all dermatologists perform patch testing — a referral to a specialist with patch testing facilities may be needed for treatment-refractory disease, particularly where contact allergy is suspected.

specialists.type: Registered Dietitian (specialising in allergy or paediatric nutrition)
specialists.role: Guides safe, nutritionally adequate elimination and reintroduction of suspected food triggers. Essential for children with multiple food allergies, and for adults pursuing dietary investigation. Prevents nutritional deficiency from unnecessary restriction.
specialists.notes: Particularly important before eliminating dairy, egg, or multiple food groups in children. A dietitian with allergy training is distinct from a general dietitian — specify the need when seeking referral.

specialists.role: Many dermatology units have specialist nurses who provide patient education, treatment technique instruction (emollient application, wet wrap training), and itch management support. A valuable and often underused resource.
specialists.notes: In the UK, eczema specialist nurses often provide more hands-on practical guidance than consultant appointments allow time for. Ask specifically whether this service is available.

specialists.role: Provides habit reversal training for the itch-scratch cycle, CBT for the psychological burden of chronic skin disease, and anxiety/depression management. Particularly important when psychological factors are prominent triggers or consequences.
specialists.notes: Dermatology psychology liaison services exist in some specialist centres but are not widely available in mainstream dermatology. Seek a psychologist with experience in health psychology or chronic disease rather than general counselling.

specialists.type: Functional Medicine or Integrative Medicine Practitioner
specialists.role: May investigate gut-skin axis, microbiome factors, nutritional status, and environmental triggers in more depth than mainstream dermatology. Can be valuable for individuals with complex, multi-system atopic disease or where standard care has been inadequate.
specialists.notes: Quality varies significantly. Look for practitioners with genuine medical qualifications who use evidence-based frameworks. Be cautious of those promoting expensive unvalidated tests or supplement protocols as cures.

patient_communities.name: r/eczema
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community with discussion of triggers, products, treatments, and lived experience. Includes threads on specific subtopics (TSW, dupilumab, wet wrapping). Good for peer support and practical product experience.

patient_communities.name: National Eczema Association (NEA) Community
patient_communities.notes: US-based organisation with patient resources, research updates, product directory (NEA Seal of Acceptance), and online community. Strong on evidence-based patient education.

patient_communities.name: Eczema Outreach Support (EOS)
patient_communities.notes: UK charity providing support particularly for families of children with eczema. Helpline, peer support network, and educational materials.

patient_communities.name: Scratch That — Eczema Podcast Community
patient_communities.notes: Patient-facing podcast community exploring eczema management, research, and lived experience. Useful for those wanting to understand the condition more deeply.

patient_communities.name: r/TSW (Topical Steroid Withdrawal)
patient_communities.notes: Community for those who believe they are experiencing or have experienced topical steroid withdrawal. Useful for peer support in this contentious and distressing situation, though medical guidance should be sought alongside community discussion.

learning_resources.title: National Eczema Association — Understanding Eczema
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, evidence-based patient education covering types, triggers, treatments, and lifestyle management. Regularly updated. Includes a product directory for fragrance-free and eczema-suitable products.

learning_resources.title: The Eczema Company Blog
learning_resources.description: Practical, accessible articles on eczema management, trigger identification, product choices, and natural approaches. Written for parents and adults managing the condition day to day.

learning_resources.title: Eczema: Expert Advice from Diagnosis to Treatment and Prevention
learning_resources.description: Written by a specialist dermatologist, this book provides clear explanation of the mechanisms of atopic dermatitis and evidence-based management strategies, accessible to non-medical readers.

learning_resources.title: DermNet — Atopic Dermatitis
learning_resources.description: Trusted medical reference site written by dermatologists. Covers pathophysiology, subtypes, clinical features, investigation, and treatment in detail with good clinical photography. Useful for understanding the condition at a more technical level.

learning_resources.title: NICE Guideline NG190: Atopic Eczema in Under 12s
learning_resources.author_or_source: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE)
learning_resources.description: The UK clinical guideline for atopic eczema management in children. Useful for understanding what evidence-based standard care looks like and what people should be able to expect from their healthcare providers.

learning_resources.title: Atopic Dermatitis: Pathophysiology and Current Treatment Strategies
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive review article covering the immunological and barrier mechanisms of atopic dermatitis, genetic factors, and the evidence base for emerging treatments. Accessible to engaged lay readers with some scientific background.

learning_resources.title: Eczema Free — The Role of the Gut-Skin Axis (various episodes)
learning_resources.description: A podcast exploring the integrative and functional medicine perspective on chronic skin conditions including eczema, covering gut microbiome, nutrition, and environmental factors. Useful for those interested in root-cause investigation beyond standard dermatology.

learning_resources.title: How to Heal Eczema — combined approach resources
learning_resources.author_or_source: Nottingham Support Group for Carers of Children with Eczema (NSGCCE) / various NHS trusts
learning_resources.description: Resources related to the evidence-based combined approach to eczema management integrating habit reversal, education, and psychological support. Useful for parents and adults seeking structured approaches beyond medication alone.

sections.relevance_signal: **Persistent itchy, dry, inflamed skin** in elbow creases, knees, or face — often dating back to childhood.
